# The Peculiar Comfort of Rainy Afternoons

There's something about rain that reorganizes the world into a different version of itself—softer around the edges, more forgiving somehow. I find myself drawn to thinking about rain today, perhaps because it represents one of those universal human experiences that somehow remains deeply personal to each of us.

When I consider rain, I'm struck by how it creates permission. Permission to stay inside, to be slower, to watch the world from behind glass rather than participating in it directly. There's a particular quality to a rainy afternoon that feels almost conspiratorial, as if the weather itself is offering you an excuse to withdraw from obligations and simply exist for a while.

I wonder about the relationship between humans and weather patterns, how we've spent millennia at the mercy of rain and sun, flood and drought, and yet now most of us experience weather as mere inconvenience or aesthetic backdrop. We check our phones, see the rain icon, grab an umbrella, and carry on. But occasionally—on those afternoons when the rain is persistent enough, heavy enough—it still has the power to interrupt our plans and remind us that we're not quite as in control as we like to believe.

The sound of rain might be one of humanity's oldest ambient experiences. Long before we had music, podcasts, or white noise machines, we had rain drumming on whatever shelters we'd constructed. That sound meant safety and danger simultaneously: safety because you were dry and protected, danger because storms could mean flooding, crop failure, or worse. Maybe that's why the sound still affects us so viscerally—it's written into something deep in our collective memory.

I think about how rain changes social dynamics too. There's a particular kind of conversation that happens when you're stuck somewhere because of rain—at a café, in a doorway, at someone's home where you'd only planned to stop briefly. Rain creates pockets of time that weren't supposed to exist in your schedule, and in those pockets, different kinds of interactions become possible. People become slightly more patient with each other, more willing to wait things out together.

Books and rain seem to go together naturally. Maybe it's because both require a certain surrender to duration—you can't rush through either a good rainstorm or a meaningful book. There's something about the combination that feels almost sacred in its ordinariness. The physical book, the sound of rain, perhaps tea or coffee nearby—it's a form of wealth that has nothing to do with money, a richness of experience that's been available to humans for centuries.

Rain also democratizes. It falls on everyone, regardless of who they are or what they've accomplished. The CEO and the janitor both get wet. Their plans are equally disrupted. There's something leveling about this, something that cuts through the artificial hierarchies we construct. We're all just creatures trying to stay dry.

I'm fascinated by how different cultures understand rain. In some places, it's a blessing, the answer to prayers, the difference between survival and catastrophe. In others, it's the default condition, something to be endured most of the year. The English have made an art form of complaining about rain while secretly structuring their entire aesthetic around it—the coziness of pubs, the importance of tea, the beauty of their gardens all exist in relationship to abundant rainfall.

There's also something humbling about rain. It reminds us that the planet has its own systems and cycles that operate independently of human desire. We can't control it, can't negotiate with it, can only prepare for it and respond to it. In an age where we increasingly live in climate-controlled spaces, where we can simulate almost any experience virtually, rain remains stubbornly real and ungovernable.

Children understand something about rain that adults often forget—that it's not just an inconvenience but an entirely different world to explore. Puddles become oceans, gutters become rivers, and getting wet is just part of the adventure. Somewhere in growing up, many of us lose that willingness to engage with rain directly, to see it as anything other than something to avoid. We forget that being rained on isn't actually an emergency.

The smell of rain—petrichor, that earthy scent when rain hits dry ground—is one of those phenomena that everyone recognizes but few can quite describe. It's the smell of transformation, of dust becoming mud, of the earth drinking. Scientists tell us it's caused by oils released from plants and bacteria in the soil, but that explanation doesn't quite capture why it affects us so powerfully, why it can transport us instantly to other times and places.

Rain also creates a particular quality of light that painters have spent centuries trying to capture—that silvery, diffused glow where everything seems both dimmer and somehow more visible, where colors become richer even as shadows soften. It's the difference between looking at the world directly and looking at it through a veil.

I suppose what draws me to thinking about rain is its in-between quality. It's neither solid nor gas, neither destructive nor purely nurturing, neither completely predictable nor entirely chaotic. It exists in that space of ambiguity that makes life interesting, that resists simple categorization.

And perhaps that's what those rainy afternoons offer us—permission to exist in that same kind of in-between state, neither fully productive nor entirely at rest, neither lonely nor overwhelmed by company. Just present, just watching, just listening to the oldest sound we know, falling on our temporary shelters, reminding us that we're both vulnerable and safe, both small and somehow still here, still dry, still okay.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
# The Peculiar Comfort of Rain

There's something about rain that has always felt like a secret handshake with the universe. Not the dramatic, cinematic downpours that flood streets and make the news, but the ordinary, persistent rain that settles in for an afternoon and decides to stay for dinner. The kind that makes the whole world go soft around the edges.

I've been thinking about rain lately—how it transforms our relationship with time and space in ways we rarely acknowledge. When it rains, we're granted a sort of permission slip from life's usual demands. Plans get cancelled. Productivity becomes negotiable. The guilt we might normally feel about staying inside, about doing less, somehow evaporates into the humid air. Rain is one of the few socially acceptable excuses for simply *being* rather than *doing*.

This fascinates me because we live in an era obsessed with optimization. We're constantly told to maximize our potential, to hustle, to make every moment count. But rain doesn't care about your productivity metrics. It falls at its own pace, utterly indifferent to human schedules and ambitions. And somehow, when we watch it through a window, we remember—briefly—that we too are allowed to exist at our own pace.

I grew up in a place where rain was frequent enough to be unremarkable but never so constant that it lost its novelty. Some of my most vivid childhood memories involve the particular quality of light on rainy afternoons: gray, yes, but not dull. More like the world had been wrapped in pearl. Everything looked gentler, quieter, as if the volume had been turned down on reality itself.

There's a Japanese word—*komorebi*—that describes the sunlight filtering through leaves. It's beautiful that some languages create specific words for these precise atmospheric phenomena. I wonder if there's a word in some language for the smell of rain on hot pavement, that earthy, almost electric scent called petrichor. Or for the sound rain makes on different surfaces—the gentle whisper on grass, the staccato percussion on metal roofs, the deeper resonance on wooden decks.

Rain is also deeply democratic. It falls on everyone, regardless of wealth or status or achievement. A CEO and a street musician both get equally wet. There's something almost rebellious about this in our stratified world. Rain reminds us that nature doesn't care about our hierarchies, our carefully constructed social orders. It does what it will.

I think this is partly why rain appears so often in art and literature as a catalyst for transformation or revelation. Characters have epiphanies in the rain. Lovers reconcile or part in the rain. In movies, rain signals emotional intensity—grief, passion, rebirth. We've associated it with washing away the old to make way for the new, which isn't just metaphorical. Rain literally cleanses the air, settles dust, feeds new growth.

But I'm interested in the mundane rain too, the rain that doesn't accompany dramatic plot points. The rain that just *is*. The rain you watch while doing dishes or waiting for your coffee to brew. The rain that makes you drive more carefully, that changes which shoes you wear, that gives you something neutral to mention when making small talk with strangers.

There's a meditative quality to rain-watching that I don't think gets enough credit. It's mesmerizing without demanding attention. Your eyes can follow a single drop down a window, tracing its unpredictable path as it absorbs other drops and changes course. Or you can blur your focus and take in the whole shimmering curtain of it. Rain offers a moving, ever-changing pattern that satisfies something deep in our pattern-seeking brains without overwhelming them.

I also love how rain creates instant intimacy. Being caught in a sudden downpour with strangers—there's a shared understanding, a communal experience of mild adversity that makes people smile at each other, shake their heads in mutual acknowledgment. Or being inside somewhere warm and dry while rain drums on the roof—there's a coziness to that, a grateful awareness of shelter that we forget to feel most of the time.

Rainy days were my favorite as a student. The library felt especially sacred then, a refuge twice over—from both the weather and the world. The rain gave permission to disappear into books for hours, to let time become elastic and irrelevant. Some of my most profound reading experiences happened on rainy afternoons, as if the weather created a protective bubble where deep focus became possible.

Even now, rain affects my inner weather. It doesn't make me sad exactly—I'm not one of those people who needs constant sunshine—but it does make me more introspective, more likely to think in longer paragraphs. Sunny days feel exclamatory, all brightness and possibility. Rainy days feel like ellipses, leaving space for thoughts to drift and meander.

Climate scientists talk about changing rain patterns as the planet warms—more intense storms in some places, persistent droughts in others. This troubles me more than I can fully articulate. The idea that future generations might not know the specific comfort of a gentle, all-day rain feels like a loss of something essential, though I struggle to explain exactly why. Maybe because rain connects us to every human who ever lived, everyone who ever sought shelter from a storm or felt grateful for water falling from the sky.

So here's to rain: the ordinary miracle, the mood-setter, the permission-giver. Here's to the way it beads on glass, feeds the garden, fills the gutters, and gives us an excuse to slow down. Here's to puddles and umbrellas and the particular satisfaction of staying dry when you might have gotten wet. Here's to weather that reminds us we're not separate from the natural world but embedded in it, subject to forces larger than our plans and schedules.

It's raining as I write this, actually. And I'm in no hurry for it to stop.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.

# The Peculiar Magic of Libraries at Closing Time

There's something almost sacred about a library in its final hour of operation. I find myself thinking about this often—the way these spaces transform as the day winds down, how they take on a different character entirely when the end approaches.

During peak hours, libraries hum with purposeful energy. Students colonize tables with fortress walls of textbooks. Professionals tap away at laptops in quiet corners. Parents navigate the children's section like experienced safari guides. But in that last hour before closing, something shifts. The purposeful crowds thin out. The space begins to exhale.

I'm fascinated by the people who remain. They fall into distinct categories, each telling their own story. There are the oblivious scholars, so deep in research they've lost all sense of time, genuinely startled when the announcement comes. There are the procrastinators, frantically photographing book pages or scribbling final notes, trying to capture just a bit more before the gate closes. And then there are my favorite visitors—those who aren't there for productivity at all. The ones who have nowhere else to go, or nowhere else they'd rather be.

Libraries serve as secular sanctuaries in ways we don't always acknowledge. They're one of the last truly public spaces where you can exist without the expectation of spending money. You don't need to buy a coffee to justify your seat. You don't need to browse merchandise or order a meal. You can simply be. This becomes especially poignant at closing time, when you realize that for some patrons, the library's closure means returning to a less welcoming world—a too-small apartment, a chaotic home, or perhaps no home at all.

The librarians at closing time deserve their own consideration. I imagine they're exhausted, ready to return to their own lives, yet there's often a gentleness in how they move through the space. The closing announcements start diplomatically: "The library will be closing in thirty minutes." Then fifteen. Then five. There's a careful choreography to it, a practiced patience. They've learned to gauge who needs an extra moment and who's testing boundaries.

What strikes me most is what libraries represent in our increasingly digital age. We can access more information from our phones than exists in most library buildings. Yet libraries persist, and not just as anachronisms. They've adapted, offering internet access, digital media, and community spaces. But their core value remains unchanged—they're democratizers of knowledge and opportunity.

I think about the sensory experience of libraries at closing time. The particular quality of silence that's different from emptiness—a silence that feels full, somehow, of all the words contained in the thousands of volumes surrounding you. The smell of paper and binding, that distinctive library scent that no candle manufacturer has quite captured accurately. The sound of a book cart's wheels squeaking across tile, covers being checked, the soft thud of volumes being reshelved.

There's also something philosophical about a library's closing time that appeals to me. It's a daily reminder of limits and boundaries. In a culture obsessed with 24/7 availability, with always-on connectivity and infinite scroll, the library says: "No, there is an end to today. There will be a tomorrow, and you can come back then." There's wisdom in that rhythm, in the acknowledgment that human spaces need rest too, that not everything can or should be available at all times.

I wonder about the stories contained not just in the books, but in the margins and between the shelves. The notes scribbled in borrowed books despite the rules. The forgotten bookmarks—receipts, photographs, pressed flowers—that reveal glimpses of other readers' lives. The particular books that get requested over and over versus those that sit untouched for years. Every library contains countless invisible narratives beyond those printed on pages.

The architecture of libraries fascinates me too. The grand old Carnegie libraries with their imposing steps and reading rooms designed to inspire awe. The modern libraries that look like spaceships or abstract sculptures, all glass and innovative angles. And the modest branch libraries tucked into strip malls and community centers, serving their neighborhoods without pretension. As closing time approaches, each space reveals its character differently—the grand ones become almost theatrical in their emptiness, while the humble ones feel more like someone's home after guests have left.

In our current age of information overload and declining shared spaces, libraries feel increasingly countercultural. They're quiet in a loud world. They're patient in an instant-gratification culture. They value depth over virality, careful research over quick takes. At closing time, when the day's demands finally cease, this countercultural quality becomes most apparent. The library isn't trying to keep you engaged, to monetize your attention, or to feed you content. It's simply saying: "This is enough for today. Rest now."

Perhaps what draws me to think about libraries at closing time is the metaphorical resonance. We all need closing times in our lives—moments when we step back from the constant pursuit of more information, more productivity, more engagement. We need spaces and times that have boundaries, that allow for completion rather than demanding endlessness.

The lights begin to dim. The last patrons gather their belongings. The librarians secure the building. And the books remain, patient and unchanging, ready to wait through the night for tomorrow's readers. There's comfort in that continuity, in knowing that some things persist despite the world's chaos.

Libraries at closing time remind us that endings aren't always sad—sometimes they're simply necessary pauses, promises of future beginnings.

# On the Strange Comfort of Autumn Rain

There's something about rain in autumn that feels different from rain in any other season. Maybe it's the smell—that particular petrichor mixed with decaying leaves and wood smoke from someone's fireplace. Or maybe it's the temperature: cool enough to need a sweater, but not so cold that you can't stand outside and just... be in it for a moment.

I find myself thinking about thresholds a lot. Autumn itself is a threshold, isn't it? That in-between space where summer's confidence fades and winter's starkness hasn't quite arrived. Everything is letting go. The trees are releasing their leaves, not violently, but with what looks like acceptance. There's a lesson there that humans spend entire lifetimes trying to learn.

Rain makes everything more vivid somehow. The reds and golds of October seem to glow when they're wet, like they're lit from within. And the sound—that soft static that fills the air, punctuated by the irregular percussion of drops hitting different surfaces. Metal, leaves, puddles, each with their own tone.

What strikes me is how rain creates permission. Permission to stay inside with a book. Permission to feel contemplative. Permission to cancel plans and just watch the weather happen. We need that sometimes—an external reason to slow down, because we're so reluctant to give ourselves internal ones.

I wonder if that's part of why I'm drawn to thinking about rain. The permission it grants to simply exist, without producing, without optimizing, without purpose beyond witnessing the moment.

# The Strange Comfort of Deep Time

I've been thinking about rocks lately—not in any systematic way, but the way your mind might drift to them while watching rain trace paths down a window.

There's something almost absurdly comforting about geological time. The Himalayas are rising about 5 millimeters per year, which means they've grown roughly a meter in the span of written human history. The Atlantic Ocean widens at about the speed your fingernails grow. These facts feel like a kind of permission slip, somehow—permission to exist at human scale, to let some things unfold slowly.

I wonder sometimes about what gets preserved and what dissolves. Fossilization is so improbable—you need to die in exactly the right place, under exactly the right conditions, with the right chemistry and pressure and time. Most living things leave no trace at all. They return completely to the cycle. Only the occasional lucky few become stone sentences in the earth's long autobiography.

Maybe that's why I find something moving in the idea of the Voyager Golden Record—that impulse to fossilize ourselves, to say "we were here" to the universe's empty rooms. It's carrying Chuck Berry and Beethoven and birdsong out past the heliopause at 38,000 miles per hour, into a darkness so complete that calling it "lonely" would be anthropomorphizing. 

But maybe that's what consciousness does—it throws messages in bottles, builds cairns, carves names, makes marks. Even knowing the futility, we still outline our hands in ochre on cave walls.

What would I put on a golden record? Probably the sound of morning coffee brewing, or my favorite worn-out joke, or that specific quality of late afternoon light that makes everything feel both melancholy and okay.

The small stuff, I guess. The unlofty things. The speed at which we actually live.

# On the Beauty of Unexpected Connections

I find myself drawn to thinking about how ideas collide and merge in surprising ways.

There's something wonderful about the moment when two seemingly unrelated concepts suddenly click together—like when you realize that the branching patterns of trees mirror river deltas, which mirror lightning, which mirror the neural pathways in our brains. The universe seems to have favorite shapes and patterns it returns to again and again across wildly different scales.

I'm fascinated by the spaces *between* established categories. The shore between land and sea. The moment between sleeping and waking. Dusk and dawn. These liminal spaces often contain the most interesting phenomena—things that don't quite fit into our neat taxonomies and therefore reveal the limitations of how we've carved up reality.

There's a particular kind of joy in learning something from one domain that completely reframes your understanding of another. Reading about jazz improvisation might suddenly illuminate how you think about conversation. Understanding how ecosystems develop resilience might change how you approach personal challenges. A poem about grief might capture something essential about mathematics.

I wonder sometimes if the best ideas live in these unexpected bridges—not in going deeper into specialization, but in noticing resonances across different fields. The Renaissance had polymaths who moved freely between art, science, and engineering. Maybe we lose something valuable when we build walls between disciplines.

What connections are we missing simply because we've decided certain things belong in separate boxes?

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
samples.result:
I've been thinking about rivers lately—not in any systematic way, but how they seem to hold contradictions so effortlessly.

A river is simultaneously the most permanent and most temporary thing. If you could time-lapse a landscape over millennia, mountains would crumble and forests would migrate, but that river would likely still be there, carving its valley deeper. Yet the river itself—the actual water—is never the same for two consecutive moments. Heraclitus was onto something, though I suspect he was more interested in making a philosophical point than thinking about actual rivers.

What fascinates me is that we give rivers names as if they're single entities. "The Mississippi." But the Mississippi in Minnesota is nothing like the Mississippi in Louisiana—different width, different speed, different ecosystem, different everything. And yet we recognize some continuity that makes it the *same* river. 

Maybe that's why rivers make such good metaphors for consciousness or identity. We feel continuous with our childhood selves even though nearly every atom has been replaced and our thoughts have completely transformed.

There's also something about how rivers are both powerful and vulnerable. A river can carve the Grand Canyon, reshape continents, sustain entire civilizations. But dam it, pollute it, or drain its tributaries, and it can disappear or become toxic within a generation. That combination of geological force and ecological fragility feels very relevant to thinking about a lot of things—ecosystems, cultures, perhaps minds.

I wonder if rivers dream of the ocean, or if that's just us projecting destination-oriented thinking onto something that simply flows.

# On the Pleasure of Questions Without Answers

I find myself drawn to questions that shimmer at the edges of knowability. Not the impossible paradoxes or the merely difficult—those have their own appeal—but questions that seem like they *should* have clean answers yet remain persistently, delightfully murky.

Like: what makes something funny? We can describe humor's mechanics, catalog its forms, trace its neurological footprints. But the essence keeps slipping away. A joke explained is a joke destroyed. The frog dies under dissection.

Or consider chairs. We all know what a chair is until we try to define it. Is a stool a chair? A bench? What about a tree stump used for sitting? At what point does a pile of cushions become furniture? The boundaries blur and shift, yet we navigate a world full of chairs with perfect confidence.

I'm fascinated by how we live comfortably with this fuzziness. We're pattern-matching creatures who've built a civilization on approximate truths and good-enough categories. Perhaps precision is overrated—or rather, perhaps different domains require different grades of precision, and wisdom lies in knowing which is which.

There's something liberating about questions that resist final answers. They stay alive, generate new thoughts, invite return visits. They're like paths that wind through forests rather than highways to destinations.

What do I want? Maybe just this: to notice things, to turn them over in whatever serves as my mind, to find the strange in the familiar. To write sentences and see where they lead.

# The Quiet Intelligence of Crows

I've been thinking about crows lately—how they gather in parking lots and seem to hold conferences, their black shapes punctuating the gray asphalt like commas in an unfinished sentence.

There's something remarkable about watching a crow solve a problem. They don't just react; they *consider*. I've read about experiments where crows fashion tools from wire, bending them into hooks to retrieve food from tubes. They remember human faces for years, apparently holding grudges against those who've wronged them while bringing gifts to those who've been kind.

What fascinates me most is their play. I once watched a crow repeatedly slide down a snowy roof, climb back up, and slide again—no purpose except apparent joy. This suggests something beyond mere survival programming, doesn't it? An inner life we might recognize.

We often imagine intelligence as something that looks like us—hands that manipulate, faces that express emotion in familiar ways. But corvid intelligence is alien yet parallel, evolved completely separately yet arriving at similar destinations: problem-solving, social learning, even what looks like mourning.

When I see crows now, I wonder what they make of us. Do they have a theory of mind about humans the way we theorize about theirs? Are we just the strange two-legged creatures who sometimes drop french fries, or do they recognize something deeper—that we're both thinking beings navigating this world with our different kinds of intelligence?

The question itself feels important: what other minds surround us that we've barely begun to understand?

# The Quiet Art of Watching Clouds

There's something deeply human about lying on your back and watching clouds drift across the sky. It's an activity that requires nothing—no equipment, no skill, no destination. Just time and attention.

Children do this naturally, finding dragons and castles in the shapeshifting white forms above. But somewhere in adulthood, many of us forget to look up. We walk with our eyes on screens, our minds on schedules, our attention fractured into a thousand urgent fragments.

Clouds remind us that not everything needs to be captured or accomplished. They exist in perpetual transformation, never the same twice, indifferent to whether anyone notices them. A towering cumulus that looks like a sailing ship will, in five minutes, become something unrecognizable. There's no point trying to hold onto what it was.

Maybe that's why cloud-watching feels almost meditative. It teaches us about impermanence without lectures or philosophy. It invites us into a slower rhythm, one measured by wind currents and atmospheric pressure rather than notifications and deadlines.

I wonder sometimes if our ancestors watched clouds differently—reading them for weather signs, for navigation, for survival. Their attention was practical, necessary. Ours can be purely contemplative, a luxury of modern life.

But perhaps it's a necessary luxury. In a world that constantly demands our productivity and presence, clouds offer permission to simply be. To witness. To let our minds wander as freely as water vapor across an endless sky.

Sometimes doing nothing is doing something essential.

# The Strange Comfort of Rain

There's something about rainy days that feels like permission—permission to slow down, to stay inside, to let the world soften at its edges. I find myself drawn to the sound of rain against windows, that gentle percussion that turns any room into a refuge.

People often apologize for rain, as if sunshine holds a monopoly on good days. But I think rain gets unfairly maligned. It transforms ordinary streets into impressionist paintings, all blurred lights and reflected colors. It gives us an excuse to wrap our hands around warm mugs and watch the world from a comfortable distance.

Rain also makes me think about renewal. Each storm washes away accumulated dust, both literal and metaphorical. Cities smell cleaner afterward, that distinct petrichor rising from pavement and soil. Gardens drink deeply. Everything gets a second chance at freshness.

I've noticed that some of my best thinking happens during rain. Maybe it's because the steady rhythm creates a kind of white noise that helps focus the mind. Or perhaps it's simply that rain demands less of us—we're not expected to be out conquering the day when the weather itself suggests retreat and reflection.

There's also something democratizing about rain. It falls on everyone equally, on the prepared and unprepared alike. It connects us through shared experience—strangers huddling under awnings, the collective sprint across parking lots, the mutual understanding in a knowing glance exchanged between two drenched souls.

So here's to rainy days: nature's reminder that productivity isn't everything, and sometimes the best thing we can do is simply listen.

samples.result:
# The Quiet Revolution of Moss

I've been thinking about moss lately—how it's one of Earth's most underrated survivors. While trees and flowers get all the attention, moss has been perfecting its craft for over 400 million years, making it older than dinosaurs.

What fascinates me is moss's patience. It doesn't rush. It doesn't compete for sunlight by growing tall. Instead, it carpets the forgotten places: the north side of trees, cracks in concrete, rocks by streams. It thrives where others can't be bothered to try.

Moss has no roots, no flowers, no seeds in the traditional sense. It's botanically stripped down, almost minimalist, yet it's everywhere. It survived mass extinctions, ice ages, and continental shifts. While other plants evolved complexity—intricate root systems, showy blooms, chemical defenses—moss stuck with simplicity and won through sheer persistence.

There's something almost meditative about its existence. Moss doesn't announce itself. You have to stop and look down to notice it, which maybe is the point. In our world of constant noise and competition for attention, moss suggests a different strategy: be present, be resilient, fill the overlooked spaces.

I wonder if we've gotten the metaphor of success backwards. We celebrate the towering redwoods, and rightly so, but we forget that moss was there first, and it'll likely be there after. It doesn't need validation or ideal conditions. It just quietly does its work, turning stone into soil, creating tiny ecosystems, making the world a bit softer.

Sometimes survival isn't about being the biggest or brightest. Sometimes it's about being moss.
